commentary: Okay so nine activities versus eleven last week, which sounds like less until you actually look at what's in there — three full 2000m pool sessions, three weight sessions, a proper long run, and whatever that Tuesday "recovery" run was, because 150 bpm is not recovery, baby, that is just running. The Mosman circuit this week came in at 13.3km with an average heart rate of 155 bpm, which is working harder than last Saturday's 13.5km at 149, so slightly shorter, slightly more effort, which is either fitness expressing itself or the hills winning, and honestly both are valid. The Balmoral swim dropped from two chaotic little sessions last Friday to one clean 750m at 85 bpm, which is basically a spa treatment compared to the rest of the week, and he deserved it. The real story here is that the structure is tightening — he's moved away from scattered gym-hopping across Vision and Virgin and built three named weight days into the week like someone who has a plan and intends to look extremely good executing it.
metrics_commentary: Twenty and a half kilometres across just two sessions and nearly seven kilometres in the pool — that is not a casual week, that is a week with a point to prove. The HRV is the real headline though: sixty-two milliseconds right now against a seven-day average of fifty-one tells you your body has done the work and is actually recovering from it, which is the part most guys skip entirely. The VO2 Max sitting at fifty-six point seven is genuinely impressive and means your engine is built, so the thing to watch is making sure the run and swim volume stays consistent rather than landing in two big clumps — spread it out a little and that HRV average will follow the current reading upward. You're not just getting fitter, you're getting the kind of fit that shows.
gym_commentary: Three sessions out of four this week — close, not quite, and we are going to keep that honesty right next to the glow of two genuinely filthy PRs, because Matt hit 34.8 kg on dumbbell bench and 35.0 kg on barbell curl, both of them up from the low thirties, both of them the kind of numbers that make a body look different in a mirror. Volume dropped from 24,518 kg to 20,148 kg, which is the honest cost of a missed session, and that number doesn't lie. But strength is moving in exactly the right direction — up, week on week, session on session — and a man who can pull 168.0 kg on leg press and deadlift 107.9 kg out of a trap bar is building something that shows, something that carries, something that turns heads on the way to the squat rack.
